title: Disc Herniation
oneLiner: Protrusion of nucleus pulposus compressing a nerve root or the cauda equina; causes radicular pain, motor/sensory deficits, or surgical emergency.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cauda equina syndrome (CES):** Acute compression of lumbosacral nerve roots below conus medullaris.
  - *Single finding:* Bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, or acute urinary retention (post-void residual > 200 mL, or inability to void).
- **Spinal cord compression (cervical/thoracic disc):** Myelopathy from central disc herniation.
  - *Single finding:* Upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski) + a sensory level on the trunk.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Dermatomal radicular pain (e.g., L4–S1 sciatica) — sharp, shooting, worse with coughing/straining
  - Lower back pain (may be secondary or absent)
  - Numbness, paraesthesia in a root distribution
  - Motor weakness (e.g., foot drop in L5, big toe extension weakness in L5)
  - **Red flags:** Bilateral leg symptoms, bladder/bowel dysfunction, gait disturbance (myelopathy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Straight leg raise (SLR):** Reproduces radicular pain at < 70° (sciatica)
  - **Contralateral SLR:** Pain in the symptomatic leg when raising the other leg (high specificity for disc herniation)
  - **Femoral nerve stretch test:** Pain in anterior thigh for L2–L4 root
  - **Reflexes:** Ankle jerk (S1) depressed; knee jerk (L4) depressed in L4 root
  - **Sensory mapping:** Pin-prick loss in dermatome
  - **Motor testing:** Ankle dorsiflexion (L4), great toe dorsiflexion (L5), ankle plantarflexion (S1)
  - **Always perform** digital rectal exam (tone, sensation) + bladder scan if red flag for CES
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Lumbar strain / mechanical back pain** — pain confined to back, no radicular spread; SLR negative
- **Spinal stenosis** — bilateral, neurogenic claudication (worse walking, relieved by sitting); often > 60 years
- **Facet joint syndrome** — unilateral pain on extension/rotation, no dermatomal loss
- **Discitis / spinal infection** — fever, raised CRP/ESR, night pain, severe tenderness to percussion
- **Epidural abscess** — triad of back pain, fever, neurological deficit; urgent MRI
- **Tumour (metastatic)** — night pain, weight loss, no improvement when supine, multiple levels
- **Hip pathology** — pain in groin/buttock, limited internal rotation, no radicular signs
- **Splitter:** Pain reproducing radicular symptoms below the knee on SLR > 60° → disc herniation; back pain only without leg radiation → mechanical back pain
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Bladder scan if urinary retention suspected (post-void residual > 200 mL)
  - Digital rectal examination for anal tone + perianal sensation
  - Gait assessment
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR — only if clinical suspicion of infection or malignancy
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line (for radicular pain with red flags or failure of conservative Rx):** MRI spine (whole spine if myelopathic signs)
  - **Second-line:** X-ray (AP + lateral) only for trauma, structural deformity, or suspected tumour (limited yield for disc)
  - **CT spine** — if MRI contraindicated (non-MRI-conditional pacemaker, etc.)
- **Special tests:**
  - Nerve conduction studies / EMG — rarely needed first-line; use if atypical presentation or to differentiate from peripheral neuropathy
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **MRI whole spine urgently (within 24 h)** if any suspicion of cauda equina syndrome or spinal cord compression — omission of imaging can lead to permanent paralysis
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If CES or cord compression:** Nil by mouth, urgent MRI, referral to on-call neurosurgery or orthopaedic spine team
  - **If radiculopathy without red flags:** Simple analgesia (see Drugs); reassure that most cases improve within 6 weeks
  - **Advice:** *Stay active* — avoid bed rest; resume normal activities as tolerated
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **If admitted for pain control:** Regular analgesics, consider neuropathic agents
  - **If waiting for surgery (CES/cord compression):** Strict monitoring for neurological deterioration
  - **Physiotherapy:** Early mobilisation with log-rolling technique
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Conservative (first-line for uncomplicated radiculopathy):** Physiotherapy (core strengthening, McKenzie exercises), acupuncture (some evidence), activity modification
  - **Epidural corticosteroid injection** — consider for severe radicular pain refractory to oral analgesia
  - **Surgery (discectomy)** — indicated for:
    - CES or cord compression (urgent)
    - Progressive motor deficit
    - Intractable pain > 6–8 weeks despite conservative therapy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil by mouth if planned for surgery; otherwise IV fluids only if dehydrated (e.g., Hartmann’s 1 L/8h)
  - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (unless surgery imminent — check local policy)
  - Diet: NBM if surgical candidate; otherwise light diet
  - Observations: baseline neuro-observations (motor, sensory, bladder) 4-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line analgesics:**
  - Paracetamol 1 g PO QDS (max 4 g/day)
  - Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO TDS (with PPI cover, e.g., omeprazole 20 mg PO OD) — avoid if eGFR < 30 or active PUD
  - **If neuropathic pain:** Gabapentin (off-label but common) — start 300 mg PO nocte, titrate up to 300 mg TDS [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Naproxen 500 mg PO BD (with PPI) — for severe radicular pain
  - Codeine phosphate 30–60 mg PO QDS (short-term; avoid if G6PD deficiency)
  - Amitriptyline 10–25 mg PO nocte (for chronic neuropathic pain, start low)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in renal impairment, asthma, GI bleed history
  - Gabapentinoids: risk of dizziness, falls; caution in elderly; renal adjustment needed
  - Codeine: avoid in patients with poor CYP2D6 metabolism (ineffective) or constipation
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gabapentin: reduce dose if eGFR < 60; [check local policy for CrCl-based dosing]
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR < 30; use with caution if < 60
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Gabapentin or pregabalin** — prescribing without renal dose adjustment can cause severe sedation, respiratory depression, or myoclonus. Check eGFR before initiating.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cauda equina syndrome (permanent bladder/bowel dysfunction, paraplegia)
  - Spinal cord compression (paralysis, sensory loss)
  - Worsening motor deficit (e.g., foot drop)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic radicular pain / failed back surgery syndrome (if surgery done)
  - Muscle wasting / weakness (e.g., longstanding foot drop)
  - Depression and disability from chronic pain
sections.Prognosis:
- **90% of acute disc herniations** improve within 6–12 weeks with conservative care; only ~10% ultimately require surgery.
- **Surgery** provides faster resolution of leg pain than conservative care, but long-term (1–2 year) outcomes are similar.
- **Cauda equina / cord compression:** prompt surgery (< 48 h) reduces but does not eliminate risk of permanent deficit; recovery is partial in > 50%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do** a rectal exam and bladder scan in anyone with bilateral leg symptoms or urinary complaints — one missed CES is a life-changing error.
- **Do** check for red flags before reassuring: age < 20 or > 55, cancer history, unexplained weight loss, fever, IVDU, recent infection.
- **Do** prescribe a PPI alongside NSAIDs in any patient > 65 or with GI risk factors.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t** send a patient home with new radicular pain and no clear safety netting — they may develop CES overnight.
- **Don’t** assume chronic back pain is ‘just’ disc disease until you have excluded inflammatory arthritis (ankylosing spondylitis) or tumour.
- **Don’t** forget to check bladder function in all patients with low back pain who have any leg neurological signs.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening leg weakness (e.g., unable to walk on heels (L5) or tip-toes (S1))
  - Loss of bladder or bowel control
  - Saddle anaesthesia (numbness in perineum, buttocks, or inner thighs)
  - Progressive pain despite simple analgesics
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP in 2 weeks if no improvement; physiotherapy referral arranged
  - If structural pathology on MRI, refer to spinal clinic (surgical or pain)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / neurosurgery on-call immediately if:**
  - Any suspicion of cauda equina syndrome or cord compression (even if imaging pending)
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially if septic from discitis/abscess)
  - Motor power < MRC 3 in a key muscle group (e.g., cannot dorsiflex ankle against gravity)
  - Lactate > 2 mmol/L if concern for sepsis (e.g., discitis/abscess)
- **Call ICU if:** GCS < 13, respiratory compromise from cervical disc (rare), or uncontrolled infection.

rawMarkdown:
## Disc Herniation
**One-liner:** Protrusion of nucleus pulposus compressing a nerve root or the cauda equina; causes radicular pain, motor/sensory deficits, or surgical emergency.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cauda equina syndrome (CES):** Acute compression of lumbosacral nerve roots below conus medullaris.
  - *Single finding:* Bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, or acute urinary retention (post-void residual > 200 mL, or inability to void).
- **Spinal cord compression (cervical/thoracic disc):** Myelopathy from central disc herniation.
  - *Single finding:* Upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski) + a sensory level on the trunk.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Dermatomal radicular pain (e.g., L4–S1 sciatica) — sharp, shooting, worse with coughing/straining
  - Lower back pain (may be secondary or absent)
  - Numbness, paraesthesia in a root distribution
  - Motor weakness (e.g., foot drop in L5, big toe extension weakness in L5)
  - **Red flags:** Bilateral leg symptoms, bladder/bowel dysfunction, gait disturbance (myelopathy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Straight leg raise (SLR):** Reproduces radicular pain at < 70° (sciatica)
  - **Contralateral SLR:** Pain in the symptomatic leg when raising the other leg (high specificity for disc herniation)
  - **Femoral nerve stretch test:** Pain in anterior thigh for L2–L4 root
  - **Reflexes:** Ankle jerk (S1) depressed; knee jerk (L4) depressed in L4 root
  - **Sensory mapping:** Pin-prick loss in dermatome
  - **Motor testing:** Ankle dorsiflexion (L4), great toe dorsiflexion (L5), ankle plantarflexion (S1)
  - **Always perform** digital rectal exam (tone, sensation) + bladder scan if red flag for CES

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Lumbar strain / mechanical back pain** — pain confined to back, no radicular spread; SLR negative
- **Spinal stenosis** — bilateral, neurogenic claudication (worse walking, relieved by sitting); often > 60 years
- **Facet joint syndrome** — unilateral pain on extension/rotation, no dermatomal loss
- **Discitis / spinal infection** — fever, raised CRP/ESR, night pain, severe tenderness to percussion
- **Epidural abscess** — triad of back pain, fever, neurological deficit; urgent MRI
- **Tumour (metastatic)** — night pain, weight loss, no improvement when supine, multiple levels
- **Hip pathology** — pain in groin/buttock, limited internal rotation, no radicular signs
- **Splitter:** Pain reproducing radicular symptoms below the knee on SLR > 60° → disc herniation; back pain only without leg radiation → mechanical back pain

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Bladder scan if urinary retention suspected (post-void residual > 200 mL)
  - Digital rectal examination for anal tone + perianal sensation
  - Gait assessment
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR — only if clinical suspicion of infection or malignancy
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line (for radicular pain with red flags or failure of conservative Rx):** MRI spine (whole spine if myelopathic signs)
  - **Second-line:** X-ray (AP + lateral) only for trauma, structural deformity, or suspected tumour (limited yield for disc)
  - **CT spine** — if MRI contraindicated (non-MRI-conditional pacemaker, etc.)
- **Special tests:**
  - Nerve conduction studies / EMG — rarely needed first-line; use if atypical presentation or to differentiate from peripheral neuropathy
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **MRI whole spine urgently (within 24 h)** if any suspicion of cauda equina syndrome or spinal cord compression — omission of imaging can lead to permanent paralysis

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If CES or cord compression:** Nil by mouth, urgent MRI, referral to on-call neurosurgery or orthopaedic spine team
  - **If radiculopathy without red flags:** Simple analgesia (see Drugs); reassure that most cases improve within 6 weeks
  - **Advice:** *Stay active* — avoid bed rest; resume normal activities as tolerated
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **If admitted for pain control:** Regular analgesics, consider neuropathic agents
  - **If waiting for surgery (CES/cord compression):** Strict monitoring for neurological deterioration
  - **Physiotherapy:** Early mobilisation with log-rolling technique
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Conservative (first-line for uncomplicated radiculopathy):** Physiotherapy (core strengthening, McKenzie exercises), acupuncture (some evidence), activity modification
  - **Epidural corticosteroid injection** — consider for severe radicular pain refractory to oral analgesia
  - **Surgery (discectomy)** — indicated for:
    - CES or cord compression (urgent)
    - Progressive motor deficit
    - Intractable pain > 6–8 weeks despite conservative therapy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil by mouth if planned for surgery; otherwise IV fluids only if dehydrated (e.g., Hartmann’s 1 L/8h)
  - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (unless surgery imminent — check local policy)
  - Diet: NBM if surgical candidate; otherwise light diet
  - Observations: baseline neuro-observations (motor, sensory, bladder) 4-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line analgesics:**
  - Paracetamol 1 g PO QDS (max 4 g/day)
  - Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO TDS (with PPI cover, e.g., omeprazole 20 mg PO OD) — avoid if eGFR < 30 or active PUD
  - **If neuropathic pain:** Gabapentin (off-label but common) — start 300 mg PO nocte, titrate up to 300 mg TDS [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Naproxen 500 mg PO BD (with PPI) — for severe radicular pain
  - Codeine phosphate 30–60 mg PO QDS (short-term; avoid if G6PD deficiency)
  - Amitriptyline 10–25 mg PO nocte (for chronic neuropathic pain, start low)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in renal impairment, asthma, GI bleed history
  - Gabapentinoids: risk of dizziness, falls; caution in elderly; renal adjustment needed
  - Codeine: avoid in patients with poor CYP2D6 metabolism (ineffective) or constipation
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gabapentin: reduce dose if eGFR < 60; [check local policy for CrCl-based dosing]
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR < 30; use with caution if < 60
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Gabapentin or pregabalin** — prescribing without renal dose adjustment can cause severe sedation, respiratory depression, or myoclonus. Check eGFR before initiating.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cauda equina syndrome (permanent bladder/bowel dysfunction, paraplegia)
  - Spinal cord compression (paralysis, sensory loss)
  - Worsening motor deficit (e.g., foot drop)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic radicular pain / failed back surgery syndrome (if surgery done)
  - Muscle wasting / weakness (e.g., longstanding foot drop)
  - Depression and disability from chronic pain

### Prognosis
- **90% of acute disc herniations** improve within 6–12 weeks with conservative care; only ~10% ultimately require surgery.
- **Surgery** provides faster resolution of leg pain than conservative care, but long-term (1–2 year) outcomes are similar.
- **Cauda equina / cord compression:** prompt surgery (< 48 h) reduces but does not eliminate risk of permanent deficit; recovery is partial in > 50%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do** a rectal exam and bladder scan in anyone with bilateral leg symptoms or urinary complaints — one missed CES is a life-changing error.
- **Do** check for red flags before reassuring: age < 20 or > 55, cancer history, unexplained weight loss, fever, IVDU, recent infection.
- **Do** prescribe a PPI alongside NSAIDs in any patient > 65 or with GI risk factors.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t** send a patient home with new radicular pain and no clear safety netting — they may develop CES overnight.
- **Don’t** assume chronic back pain is ‘just’ disc disease until you have excluded inflammatory arthritis (ankylosing spondylitis) or tumour.
- **Don’t** forget to check bladder function in all patients with low back pain who have any leg neurological signs.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening leg weakness (e.g., unable to walk on heels (L5) or tip-toes (S1))
  - Loss of bladder or bowel control
  - Saddle anaesthesia (numbness in perineum, buttocks, or inner thighs)
  - Progressive pain despite simple analgesics
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP in 2 weeks if no improvement; physiotherapy referral arranged
  - If structural pathology on MRI, refer to spinal clinic (surgical or pain)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / neurosurgery on-call immediately if:**
  - Any suspicion of cauda equina syndrome or cord compression (even if imaging pending)
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially if septic from discitis/abscess)
  - Motor power < MRC 3 in a key muscle group (e.g., cannot dorsiflex ankle against gravity)
  - Lactate > 2 mmol/L if concern for sepsis (e.g., discitis/abscess)
- **Call ICU if:** GCS < 13, respiratory compromise from cervical disc (rare), or uncontrolled infection.
